channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: A Fragment Calculus for Cross-Discipline Claims
item.description:
The problem

Research programs that move between math, architecture, and implementation suffer from illicit globalism: something proven “in math mode” quietly becomes a design invariant in “runtime mode,” then becomes a test oracle in “implementation mode,” even though each move silently changed assumptions. The question is not “is claim cc true?” but “in which fragments does cc survive closure, and what’s the delta cost to make it survive elsewhere?”

Primitives

Fragment. A fragment is a container for reasoning under controlled assumptions:

item.title: Environment and Totality: The Local and the Whole
item.description:
Introduction: From Here to Everything

Two companion surveys in this collection treat environment and totality separately. The environment survey traces what surrounds a thing — from Aristotle’s topos through Uexküll’s Umwelt, Gibson’s affordances, and process-relational accounts where organism and world co-constitute each other. The totality survey examines the whole — mereological sums, set-theoretic paradoxes, Whitehead’s creative advance, and relational ontologies that refuse to totalize.

This survey asks the question that connects them: how does the local become the whole? An organism has an environment. That environment sits within a larger environment. At some point the nesting must either terminate in a totality or dissolve into something that resists closure. The transition from local to global is where the deepest problems lie — problems of context, nesting, boundary, and scale.

item.title: Action and Change: A Survey of Substance and Process Accounts
item.description: What is an action? What is change? These questions matter because they concern the most basic features of the world we inhabit. Everything we observe involves change—seasons shift, bodies age, thoughts emerge and fade. Everything we do involves action—we choose, move, speak, create. Yet philosophy has produced not one coherent account but a fundamental divergence between two pictures: substance philosophy, which asks “what ARE things, and what do they DO?” and process philosophy, which asks “how do things BECOME, and is activity more fundamental than being?”

item.title: Philosophy of Environment: From Aristotle's Place to Relational Ontologies
item.description:
Introduction: The Problem of Environment

What is an environment? The question seems obvious until you press it. An animal has an environment — the forest around it, the nest it lives in, the temperature and light. A human has an environment — the city, the home, the workplace. But what exactly is the relation between the thing and what surrounds it?

Two traditions in Western philosophy have tried to answer this. The first, stretching from Aristotle through Locke and Russell, treats environment as external: a collection of things, conditions, or properties that surround a thing and shape how it behaves. Environment is what is not the thing itself. There is a boundary, and on one side is the thing; on the other side is everything else.

item.title: Quality and Property: A Survey of Substance and Process Accounts
item.description:
Introduction

Every thing possesses qualities. A rock is hard, heavy, gray. A promise is solemn, binding, abstract. A person is brave, measured, weary. Qualities are how things become distinguishable — how we tell one thing from another, how we describe what we encounter.

Yet “quality” is philosophically fraught. Is a quality a universal (the property of redness that many things share) or a particular (this specific redness here, unrepeatable)? Is it observable (color, temperature) or intrinsic (essence, nature)? Does a quality exist in the thing itself, or only in the mind of an observer? Does a thing have qualities as a substance has attributes, or does a thing consist of the qualities it realizes at a moment?

item.title: Relation and Identity: A Survey of Substance and Process Accounts
item.description:
I. The Problem

What is a relation? Is it a fundamental feature of reality, or a projection of our conceptual schemes? Does identity rest on intrinsic properties, or is it constituted by relational structure? How does something remain itself through change?

These questions are ancient. They arise wherever we try to say what reality is. Aristotle distinguished relatives — things that are what they are only in relation to something else. Russell fought against Bradley’s regress, which threatened to make relations incoherent. Leibniz grounded identity in indistinguishability across all properties and relations. Whitehead inverted the entire tradition: things are not primary; relations are. Nāgārjuna, writing seventeen centuries before Whitehead, argued that nothing has intrinsic nature — all entities arise through dependent origination, through relations to other entities.

item.title: State, Time, and Event: A Survey of Substance and Process Accounts
item.description:
State, time, and event form a cluster tight enough that you cannot define one without the others. A state is a configuration at a moment. An event is a transition between states. Time is what makes both state and change possible. These three are among the most disputed concepts in philosophy: they are fundamental to metaphysics, yet remain deeply puzzling.

Why does this matter? Because every coherent worldview must answer: What persists and what changes? How do we distinguish a thing at one moment from itself at another? What is the relationship between a thing’s enduring identity and the flow of temporal experience? Is time real at all, or is it illusion?

item.title: Substance and Process: Two Traditions in Ontology
item.description:
Philosophy has always struggled with a fundamental question: what is real — stable being or constant becoming? Two incompatible traditions have emerged from this struggle, and they reach opposite conclusions.

Substance philosophy answers: reality is made of things — persistent entities with properties and natures. Aristotle, Locke, Leibniz, Russell: these philosophers ground reality in being, in what things ARE, in their intrinsic character and identity.

Process philosophy answers: reality is made of events and becomings — actualization, activity, creative emergence. Whitehead, Bergson, Peirce, Deleuze: these philosophers ground reality in activity, in what things DO, in their continuous creative unfolding.

item.title: Totality and Mereology: The Problem of the Whole and All-That-Is
item.description:
Introduction: The Impossible Whole

What is everything? The question seems to demand an answer: the totality of all things, the whole that contains all parts, the sum of all that exists. But this answer conceals a deep problem. As soon as you try to define everything precisely, you run into paradoxes. The set of all sets, if it exists, either contains itself (contradiction) or does not contain itself (also contradiction). There is no largest infinity; for any infinite cardinal, there is a larger one. The whole that would contain itself exceeds the very logic we use to define wholes.

item.title: Spiral Research
item.description:
A practice for designing things that must be simultaneously grounded in:

  • existing standards and prior art (external)
  • the project’s own archive and constraints (internal)
  • the current state of formal planning (prospective)

The practice is recursive. Each cycle produces a more detailed plan that becomes the input to the next cycle.

The Cycle

1. Check the leading edge of planning docs

Before researching externally, read the most recent planning output. What do we already know? What are the open questions? What have we committed to? What is still undecided?
